figures.caption: Figure 1: Overset-grid interpolation framework. (a) Global view of the curvilinear overset grids for flow simulations over an airfoil; the body-conforming (O-grid) is shown in blue, whereas the background Cartesian grid in red. The fringe (receiver) points on the background grid are identified by the red nodes. (b) An overset interface showing the donor (blue) and receiver (red) grids and the curvilinear mapping used for overset interpolation.
Figure 2: Schematic of the one-dimensional overlapping grids on which Eq. (16) is solved.

Figure 3: Schematic of a wing undergoing rigid-body motion. The right side shows the rotational sequence ending at (X3, Y3, Z3), while the left side shows the complete translation and rotation. The dashed and solid wings denote the initial (at time t0) and translated/rotated configuration, respectively.

Figure 4: 1-D scalar advection results at t = 0.5 for various schemes: (a) & (b) 1–2–1 scheme, (c) & (d) 2–4–2 scheme, and (e) & (f) 3–6–3 scheme. The left column shows the solutions, while the right column shows the spatial errors. The vertical dashed line indicates the overset interface of the moving middle grid.
Figure 5: (a) Solution from the 3 − 6 − 3 scheme at three times; the vertical dashed lines mark the moving overset interface. (b) Convergence plot verifying the global accuracy.
Figure 6: Eigenvalue spectra of the system matrix (31) evaluated at the solution time t = 0.5 for the (a) 1−2−1, (b) 2−4−2, and (c) 3− 6− 3 schemes.
Figure 7: 2-D vortex convection initial grid: (a) a square background grid (black) overlapping a smaller grid (red) which rotates with time, and (b) the initial density contour.
Figure 8: Density contours at two different time instants for the 2-D isentropic vortex convection on the moving overset configuration.

Figure 9: Log–log convergence plots of ∥ερ∥∞. (a) Comparison of the errors from linear and cubic interpolation with 3− 6− 3 scheme. (b) Comparison of the errors from 1− 2− 1, 2− 4− 2, and 3− 6− 3 schemes using Lagrange cubic interpolation.
Figure 10: Density error contours using the 3−6−3 scheme on (a) Resolution 1 and (b) Resolution 4 gird. (c) Density contours showing the location of the isentropic vortex at that solution time.
Figure 11: A comparison of ∥ερ∥∞ from the strong and weak overset treatment applied to solve the 2-D vortex convection problem on a moving overset configuration shown in Fig. 7.
Figure 13: 2-D pitching NACA 0012 airfoil for ψ0 = 0◦: (a) moving overset grid configuration and (b) z-vorticity field with contour levels in the range ωz ∈ [−0.3, 0.3].
Figure 14: 2-D pitching NACA 0012 airfoil for ψ0 = 11◦: (a) moving overset grid configuration and (b) z-vorticity field with contour levels in the range ωz ∈ [−0.3, 0.3].
Figure 15: Validation of the lift coefficient CL for the (a) ψ0 = 0◦ and (b) for ψ0 = 11◦ pitching airfoil/wing flow cases against the results of [39].
Figure 16: Flow past a pitching 3-D NACA 0012 wing for ψ0 = 11◦: (a) moving overset grid configuration and (b) iso-surfaces of vorticity magnitude at |ω| = 0.3, 0.5, 1.0, colored by x-momentum.
Figure 17: z-vorticity contours at two different time instants in the flow past a 2-D heaving circular cylinder.

Figure 18: 3-D wake visualization for a transversely oscillating (heaving) circular cylinder at ReD = 185 and fo/fvs = 1.0. Iso-surfaces of vorticity magnitude, |ω| = 0.3, 0.5, 1.0, are shown and colored by x-momentum.
Figure 19: Lift coefficient (CL) response of a heaving circular cylinder at fo/fvs = 1.0.

Figure 20: Flow past a rotating circular cylinder at ReD = 200: (a) zoomed in instantaneous z-vorticity contours around the overset interface illustrating the asymmetric wake induced by cylinder rotation; (b) time history of the lift coefficient CL compared with reference results.
Figure B.21: Grid convergence results showing ∥ερ∥∞ from the proposed overset scheme for the 2-D isentropic vortex convection problem solved over static overset grids.
Figure D.23: Circular cylinder at ReD = 500: (a) instantaneous z-vorticity (ωz) contour field, and (b) time history of the lift coefficient, CL.
Figure C.22: NACA 0012 airfoil at α = 10◦ and Rec = 1000: (a) instantaneous z-vorticity (ωz) contour and (b) time history of the lift coefficient, CL.
